I want to record and release a cover of a Beyoncé song. What licenses do I need and how do I get them? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Nia","genre":"R&B","monthlyListeners":50000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"licensing"}]

Nia, recording a cover song is one of the few situations where you have a near-automatic right to license — thanks to the compulsory mechanical license under US copyright law. Here's the process.

What You Need: A Mechanical License
A mechanical license grants you the right to reproduce and distribute someone else's composition in a new recording. For cover songs, US copyright law (Section 115) gives you a compulsory right to this license — meaning the publisher CANNOT refuse you, as long as:
1. The song has been previously commercially released
2. You don't change the fundamental character of the song (no parody, no major lyric changes)
3. You pay the statutory royalty rate

The 2026 Statutory Mechanical Rate:
- Physical/download: ~$0.12 per copy for songs under 5 minutes
- Streaming: Handled via the MLC blanket license (your distributor covers this automatically)

How to Get the License:

Option 1 — Through Your Distributor (Easiest):
Many distributors handle cover song licensing automatically:
- DistroKid: Cover song licensing built in (additional $12/year per cover)
- CD Baby: Partners with Easy Song Licensing
- TuneCore: Partners with HFA/The MLC
Your distributor handles the paperwork and pays the statutory rate on your behalf.

Option 2 — Direct Through a Licensing Service:
- Easy Song Licensing (easysong.com): $15.95 per song + statutory royalties
- Harry Fox Agency (HFA): Traditional mechanical licensing clearinghouse
- Songfile by HFA: Online self-service for mechanical licenses

Option 3 — Direct from the Publisher:
You can contact the publisher directly. Search ASCAP ACE or BMI Repertoire databases to find who publishes the Beyoncé song, then request a mechanical license. This is more work but sometimes necessary for custom terms.

What You Do NOT Need:
- A master recording license (you're making your OWN recording, not using Beyoncé's)
- A sync license (unless you're making a music video — that's a separate license)
- Publisher approval (compulsory license means they cannot refuse)

What You CANNOT Do Under Compulsory License:
- Change the lyrics substantially
- Change the fundamental melody or character of the song
- Use it in a commercial/advertisement (that requires a DIRECT sync license, not compulsory)

For YouTube/Music Video:
If you want to upload a music video of your cover to YouTube, you need BOTH a mechanical license (for the audio) AND a sync license (for pairing audio with video). YouTube's Content ID system may automatically monetize your video and pay the original publisher.

I recommend Option 1 through your distributor — it's the fastest and handles all the royalty accounting automatically. Want me to check the publisher info for the specific Beyoncé song?

What's the difference between blanket licensing and direct licensing? Which one makes me more money? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Elle","genre":"Indie","monthlyListeners":70000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"licensing"}]

Elle, understanding blanket vs. direct licensing is fundamental to maximizing your income. Here's the complete comparison.

Blanket License:
A blanket license gives the licensee access to an ENTIRE catalog of music for a fixed fee or percentage of revenue. They don't need to negotiate individual song rights.

Who Uses Blanket Licenses:
- Radio stations (pay ASCAP/BMI a blanket fee for unlimited plays)
- Streaming platforms (Spotify, Apple Music pay the MLC a blanket fee for mechanicals)
- Restaurants, retail stores (pay PROs for background music)
- Some fitness platforms

How You Earn: Through your PRO (performance royalties) and the MLC (mechanical royalties). You do NOT negotiate these individually — they are pooled and distributed based on usage data.

Direct License:
A direct license is negotiated between you and a specific user for a specific use of a specific song.

Who Uses Direct Licenses:
- Film/TV productions (sync licenses)
- Advertisers (commercial sync)
- Video game developers
- Podcast producers
- Brand campaigns
- Any use where the user wants a specific song

How You Earn: A negotiated fee (sync fee) plus backend performance royalties through your PRO.

Which Makes More Money?

| Scenario | Better Option | Why |
|----------|--------------|-----|
| Background music in stores/restaurants | Blanket | High volume, low per-use value. PRO handles collection. |
| Song in a TV commercial | Direct | High per-use value. You set the price ($5K-$100K+). |
| Streaming on Spotify | Blanket | Automatic via distributor/MLC. No negotiation needed. |
| Song in a Netflix film | Direct | Premium placement. Negotiate $10K-$50K+ directly. |
| Songs in a fitness app | Either | Blanket for broad access, Direct for featured placement. |
| Song in a video game | Direct | One-stop negotiation, higher fee per placement. |

The Strategy:
You should have BOTH working simultaneously:
1. Blanket: Your PRO and MLC registrations ensure you collect from ALL blanket-licensed use (streaming, radio, venues) automatically.
2. Direct: For high-value sync opportunities (film, TV, ads, games), negotiate directly to maximize per-placement revenue.

The key insight: blanket licensing is your PASSIVE income floor. Direct licensing is your active revenue ceiling. Maximize both.

A coffee shop asked if they need to pay me to play my album in their store. How does music licensing work for businesses? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Great question — and the answer involves the blanket licensing system that generates billions in music royalties. Here's how it works:

The law: Any business that plays music publicly (shops, restaurants, bars, gyms, offices) needs a public performance license. Playing Spotify through speakers in a business is a public performance, even though the business has a Spotify subscription. Spotify's terms of service explicitly state that personal subscriptions are not licensed for commercial use.

How businesses legally play music: They purchase blanket licenses from the PROs: ASCAP: annual license for businesses (cost varies by business size, type, and square footage. A small coffee shop might pay $300-$800/year). BMI: similar pricing. SESAC: similar, but smaller catalog. A blanket license gives the business permission to play ANY song in that PRO's catalog. Some businesses also use commercial streaming services: Soundtrack Your Brand (Spotify's commercial offering). Cloud Cover Music. Rockbot. These services include the blanket license in their subscription fee.

How you get paid: When the coffee shop pays ASCAP $500/year for their blanket license, that money goes into ASCAP's general pool. ASCAP distributes royalties based on a combination of: streaming data, radio play data, survey data, and cue sheet data. For indie artists, the per-play royalty from a single coffee shop is microscopic — fractions of a cent. BUT these royalties compound across thousands of businesses.

Should the coffee shop pay YOU directly? No — the blanket license system means they pay the PRO, not individual artists. You earn your share through your PRO membership.

What YOU need to do: Ensure your songs are registered with your PRO (ASCAP or BMI). Use a PRO that actively monitors business performances (both ASCAP and BMI do this). Consider registering with both a PRO and SoundExchange for different types of public performance income.

If the business asks you for a DIRECT license (bypassing the PRO system): this is unusual for background music but can happen for featured use (a fitness studio wanting to build a class around your music). In that case, negotiate a direct sync-style license.

How do I get my music placed in TV shows and movies? I'm fully independent with no label or publisher. [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"hasAudio":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"indie-folk"}}]

Independent artists have a massive advantage in sync licensing: you're a one-stop shop. Music supervisors love that because it means one agreement instead of negotiating with a label AND a publisher. Here's your strategy:

**The Sync Pipeline for Independents:**

1. **Own Everything:** Confirm you control both the master recording AND the publishing (composition). As a fully independent artist, you likely do. This makes you a 'one-stop clear' — the single biggest advantage in sync.

2. **Build a Sync-Ready Catalog:**
   - Instrumental versions of every track (supervisors often need vocals removed)
   - Clean versions if you have explicit content
   - Stems available (vocals, drums, bass, melody — separated)
   - Metadata: BPM, key, mood tags, lyric themes

3. **Reach Music Supervisors:**
   - **Direct pitching:** Research who supervises your target shows (IMDb, end credits, social media). Follow them, engage with their work, send targeted pitches.
   - **Sync agents/representatives:** Companies like Musicbed, Marmoset, or Artlist represent indie artists. They pitch on your behalf for a commission (typically 25-50% of the sync fee).
   - **Sync libraries:** Platforms like Songtradr, Epidemic Sound, or Pond5 where supervisors browse. Lower fees but higher volume.

4. **The Pitch:**
   - Short, specific: 'I have a warm acoustic folk track (120 BPM, G major) that would fit [show name] scenes about [themes].'
   - Include a private streaming link (not a download — you want to track plays)
   - Never attach MP3s to cold emails (spam filters + unprofessional)

**Fee Expectations for Independent Artists (2026):**
- Major network TV show: $5,000-25,000 (master + sync)
- Streaming series (Netflix, Hulu): $3,000-15,000
- Indie film: $500-5,000
- Reality TV: $1,000-3,000
- Trailer: $10,000-50,000+

As an independent, you keep 100% of these fees. A label-signed artist typically splits the master fee with their label.

How do I get my music into video games? I play a lot of games with great soundtracks and I think my music could fit. [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Video game sync is one of the fastest-growing licensing markets and it's MORE accessible to indie artists than film/TV sync. Here's why and how:

Why games are great for indie music: (1) Games use MORE music than movies — a single game might license 50-200 songs. (2) Indie aesthetic is valued — many games specifically want underground music for authenticity. (3) Games have long shelf lives — a song in a game earns royalties for YEARS as the game continues selling. (4) Massive exposure — popular games have millions of players hearing your music.

Types of game music placements: (1) Licensed soundtrack: your existing recording is used in the game (like GTA, FIFA, Need for Speed, Tony Hawk). This requires both a master and sync license. (2) In-game radio: your song plays on a fictional radio station within the game world. (3) Trailers/marketing: your music is used in game trailers and ads (often highest-paying). (4) Rhythm games: Beat Saber, Fortnite Festival, etc. — these have dedicated music licensing teams.

How to approach game studios: (1) Identify upcoming games in genres that match your music. Follow game studios on social media, subscribe to industry outlets (GameSpot, IGN, Game Developer). (2) Find the music supervisor or audio director. AAA studios have in-house music supervisors. Indie game developers often handle music themselves. LinkedIn is the best tool for finding the right contact. (3) Prepare your pitch: a short curated playlist (not your entire catalog — 5-8 tracks). Stems/instrumentals available. A one-paragraph intro about your sound and aesthetic. Your music on streaming platforms for easy preview.

Submission platforms: Songtradr (has specific game licensing categories). Musicbed (curated, some game clients). Artlist (growing game client base). Direct submission to game studio websites (many have music submission forms).

Licensing fees: Indie games: $500-$5,000 per song. AAA games: $5,000-$50,000+ per song. Game trailers: $10,000-$100,000+. These are wide ranges — negotiation depends on the game's budget, your profile, and the scope of use.

What to have ready: (1) Instrumentals (mandatory — many games need music without vocals). (2) Stems (separated tracks — some games need interactive audio). (3) Clean versions (no explicit language for games targeting younger audiences). (4) 30-second, 60-second, and 90-second edits. (5) Clear rights documentation — confirm you own master AND publishing (or have authority to license both).

I keep getting DMs from YouTubers and podcasters asking to use my music. Should I let them? Should I charge? How does this work? [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

This is actually a growing market that many indie artists monetize effectively. Content creator licensing sits between 'free exposure' and 'full sync licensing.' Here's how to handle it:

The hierarchy of content creator requests: (1) Small creators (<10k subscribers): likely can't afford a license fee. Consider: granting free use with mandatory credit (link to your Spotify/social in their description). Why: genuine exposure to their audience, goodwill, and your music reaching new ears. (2) Mid-tier creators (10k-100k): can afford $25-$100 per song per video. Create a simple licensing form on your website or use a platform like SongFinch or Musicbed. (3) Large creators (100k-1M+): charge $100-$500+ per song. Negotiate based on: how many views they average, how prominently your music is featured, and whether it's a one-time use or blanket license. (4) Enterprise creators/companies (1M+ or brand accounts): $500-$5,000+ per song. These are real sync licenses.

The micro-licensing structure: A simple agreement should cover: what song(s) are being licensed, what platform(s) the content will appear on, whether the license is exclusive or non-exclusive, the duration (perpetual or time-limited), the fee (or 'gratis with credit'), and what attribution is required.

Platforms that automate this: Epidemic Sound, Artlist, Musicbed — join as an artist and let the platform handle licensing. They take 30-50% but eliminate the administrative headache. Lickd — specifically for YouTube creators licensing real music. Content ID considerations: if your music is registered with Content ID (via your distributor), a creator using your music without a license will trigger a claim. You can: whitelist specific videos/channels manually through your distributor, or issue Content ID waivers to creators who've licensed your music.

The 'free use' debate: Giving free licenses isn't always bad IF: the creator links to your Spotify/Apple Music. The creator has an audience that matches your target demographic. You get the content for YOUR social media too (cross-promotion). Don't give free licenses if: the creator won't credit you, your music is the primary draw of the content (they need YOUR sound specifically — that has value), or it's a commercial/brand-sponsored video (they have budget).

Template response for DMs: 'Hey! Thanks for reaching out — I appreciate you wanting to use my music! For creators under 10k followers, I'm happy to grant free use as long as you credit me with a link to [your Spotify]. For larger channels, I offer affordable micro-licenses starting at $50. Let me know your channel size and I can get you set up!'

A production company in Germany wants to license my music for a European TV series. Is it different from licensing in the US? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"electronic"}}]

International sync has significant differences from US licensing. Here's what you need to know:

**Key Differences in European Sync:**

1. **Collective Management is Stronger:** In Germany, GEMA (the local PRO) collects sync royalties more aggressively than US PROs. The production company may need to clear the sync THROUGH GEMA, not just with you directly.

2. **Mechanical Royalties on Sync:** In Europe, some territories require a separate mechanical license for sync usage (the 'reproduction right'). In the US, sync licenses typically bundle this. Confirm whether the European license covers both sync AND mechanical.

3. **Territory Definition:** 'European rights' is vague. Specify: EU only? EU + UK? Pan-European including non-EU states? Each territory adds value and should increase the fee.

4. **Currency:** Negotiate in USD or specify the exchange rate date. EUR/USD fluctuation can cost you 5-15% over a payment timeline.

5. **Backend Royalties:** European broadcasters generally pay higher performance royalties per broadcast than US networks. Your PRO must have reciprocal agreements with the local European PROs (GEMA for Germany, SACEM for France, PRS for UK) to collect.

**What You Need:**
- Sub-publisher or publishing administrator with European coverage (Songtrust, TuneCore Publishing, or a direct European sub-publishing deal). Without this, you'll miss backend royalties from European broadcasts.
- Confirm your PRO has reciprocal agreements to collect from the relevant territories.

**Fee Adjustment:** European TV sync fees are generally 60-80% of US equivalents for comparable shows. A German network series might pay €3,000-8,000 where a US network show pays $5,000-15,000.

For publishing administration setup in European territories, route to Publishing via indii Conductor.

A clothing brand wants to use my music in their social media campaigns and potentially create a branded playlist. What kind of deal should I negotiate? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Kai","genre":"R&B/Streetwear Culture","monthlyListeners":130000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"licensing"}]

Kai, brand partnerships are one of the highest-value licensing opportunities for independent artists. Here's how to structure this deal.

Two Separate Licensing Components:

1. Social Media Music Use (Sync License):
The brand wants to sync your music to their video content on social platforms. This requires:
- A sync license from you (composition + master) for each song used
- Specify: which platforms (Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, all?), how many posts/videos, for how long, and whether the brand can boost/promote the posts as paid ads

Fee Structure for Social Media Sync:
- Organic social only (no paid boost): $2,000-$8,000 per song, per platform, per quarter
- Including paid media/ads: $5,000-$25,000+ per song (paid ads dramatically increase impressions)
- All platforms bundle: Multiply the single-platform rate by 2-3x

2. Branded Playlist (No Sync Required — But Leverage!):
- A branded Spotify playlist featuring your music does NOT require a sync license (Spotify's blanket license covers playback)
- However, this is still valuable exposure — negotiate it as part of the overall deal
- Ask for: playlist promotion commitment (social posts, newsletter mentions), guaranteed playlist positioning, minimum playlist duration

Negotiation Framework:

| Component | Low End | Mid Range | Premium |
|-----------|---------|-----------|--------|
| 1 song, organic social, 1 platform, 3 months | $2,000 | $5,000 | $10,000 |
| 1 song, paid ads, all platforms, 6 months | $8,000 | $15,000 | $30,000 |
| 3 songs, paid+organic, all platforms, 1 year | $20,000 | $40,000 | $75,000 |

Key Terms to Negotiate:
1. Exclusivity: Does the brand want to be the ONLY brand using your music? If yes, charge 2-3x the standard rate.
2. Territory: Worldwide vs. US-only vs. specific markets
3. Modifications: Can they edit, remix, or create derivatives of your music?
4. Credit: Require artist credit in all posts, with link to your streaming profile
5. Content ownership: Who owns the branded content after the campaign ends?
6. Performance royalties: You still earn PRO royalties from any public performance of your music in their content

At 130K monthly listeners with R&B/streetwear alignment, you have genuine cultural value to a clothing brand. Start negotiations at the mid-range and negotiate down only if necessary. I can draft the sync license terms once you've agreed on pricing.

A record label wants to include my song on a compilation album. What kind of license is this and what should I charge? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Jules","genre":"Pop","monthlyListeners":80000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"licensing"}]

Jules, compilation album licensing involves two separate rights that the label needs from you.

What They Need:

1. Master Use License: Permission to use YOUR specific recording on the compilation. Since you own your masters, you grant this directly.

2. Mechanical License: Permission to reproduce the underlying composition. If you are the sole songwriter, you grant this too. If there are co-writers, all composition owners must agree.

Fee Structure:

Physical Compilations (CD/Vinyl):
- Flat fee per unit manufactured: $0.05-$0.15 per unit
- OR fixed advance against per-unit royalty: $500-$3,000 advance
- Statutory mechanical rate applies to the composition

Digital/Streaming Compilations:
- Flat license fee: $500-$2,000 for inclusion
- Or pro-rata share: you receive your proportional streaming royalties
- Most legitimate labels offer a per-stream pro-rata share

Key Terms to Negotiate:
1. Non-exclusive: Your track should NOT be exclusive to this compilation. You should retain the right to release it independently and license it elsewhere.
2. Term: 3-5 years maximum. Do not grant perpetual.
3. Territory: Specify regions (worldwide or limited).
4. Digital distribution: Ensure the compilation is distributed properly so your PRO and MLC can track plays.
5. Credit: Full artist name and track title in all materials.
6. Promotional use: Limit their right to use your name/likeness in compilation promotion.

Red Flags:
- They ask for exclusive rights to your track
- No upfront payment AND no per-unit/stream royalty
- Perpetual term
- They want you to PAY to be included (this is a vanity compilation — avoid)

For a legitimate compilation at your level, I'd target a $1,000-$2,000 flat fee plus pro-rata streaming revenue. Want me to draft the license?

I found a Creative Commons track I want to sample. What are my rights? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true}]

Creative Commons has 6 license types — they're NOT all the same:

- **CC BY** — Use freely, just credit the creator
- **CC BY-SA** — Use freely, credit, and share your work under the same license
- **CC BY-NC** — Attribution required, NO commercial use
- **CC BY-ND** — Attribution required, no modifications (can't sample)
- **CC0** — Public domain, no restrictions

I need to check which specific CC license applies. Running `check_availability` with the source URL. The 'NC' and 'ND' variants are the ones that cause problems for commercial releases.

I love sampling but clearance is expensive. Are there cheaper or easier ways to legally use elements from other songs? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Dex","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":55000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"licensing"}]

Dex, great question — there are several legitimate alternatives to traditional sample clearance that are faster, cheaper, and still legally sound.

1. Tracklib (Best for Authentic Sampling):
- A platform specifically designed for legal sampling
- Browse and preview real songs from real artists (not loops)
- Three pricing tiers based on sample prominence:
  - Category C (minor sample): ~$100 + 2% royalty share
  - Category B (medium sample): ~$250 + 15% royalty share
  - Category A (major/recognizable sample): ~$500 + 20% royalty share
- Clearance is INSTANT — no waiting for publisher approval
- You receive a license certificate immediately
- Best for: Hip-hop producers who want authentic samples with fast clearance

2. Splice / Sample Packs (Pre-Cleared Loops):
- Millions of royalty-free loops, one-shots, and stems
- Once downloaded, you can use them commercially with no royalty obligations
- Cost: $9.99-$29.99/month subscription
- Limitation: Not actual songs — they're purpose-built loops. Less authentic than sampling a record.
- Read EACH pack's license carefully — some have restrictions on exclusivity

3. Interpolation (Re-Performing):
- Instead of sampling the actual recording, replay the elements you want to use
- You only need to clear the PUBLISHING (composition) rights, not the master
- Usually cheaper: 10-30% of publishing vs. 10-50% publishing + master license fee
- You maintain full ownership of your master recording
- Best for: When the melodic idea is what you want, not the specific sound

4. Public Domain Sampling:
- Songs published before 1928 are in the public domain in the US
- You can sample ANY recording of a public domain composition that was itself recorded before 1928
- For newer recordings of PD compositions: you still need master clearance for the specific recording, but the composition is free

5. Creative Commons Music:
- Some artists release music under CC licenses that permit sampling
- Free Music Archive, ccMixter, and Internet Archive are sources
- Check the specific CC license: CC BY allows commercial use, CC BY-NC does not

6. Direct Artist Licensing:
- Reach out directly to independent artists whose music you want to sample
- Indie artists are often MORE willing to license at reasonable rates than major label artists
- You can sometimes negotiate a flat fee ($500-$2,000) instead of royalty splits

Cost Comparison:
| Method | Upfront Cost | Ongoing Royalty | Speed |
|--------|-------------|----------------|-------|
| Traditional clearance | $2,000-$50,000+ | 25-50% publishing | 2 weeks-6 months |
| Tracklib | $100-$500 | 2-20% | Instant |
| Splice | $10-$30/month | 0% | Instant |
| Interpolation | $500-$5,000 negotiation | 10-30% publishing | 1-4 weeks |
| Public domain | $0 | 0% | Instant |

Want me to search for specific songs or elements on Tracklib or Splice?

A sync agency wants to represent my catalog exclusively for 3 years. They take 50% of any placements they land. The alternative is staying non-exclusive on Songtradr where I keep 65%. Which is better? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"electronic"}}]

This is one of the most consequential decisions in sync licensing. Let me analyze the contract terms and model both scenarios.

**Option A — Exclusive Sync Agency (50% commission, 3-year term):**

✅ **What you get:**
- Active pitching by a dedicated team with relationships (this is HUGE — supervisors trust their known agents)
- They do the outreach, negotiation, and paperwork
- Potentially higher-value placements through established connections
- Some agencies provide upfront catalog evaluation and tagging

❌ **What you give up:**
- 50% of every placement fee (industry standard is 30-40% — 50% is high)
- EXCLUSIVE means you cannot pitch your own music for sync during the term
- EXCLUSIVE means you cannot use Songtradr, Musicbed, or any other platform
- If they don't actively pitch your catalog, your music sits dormant for 3 years
- You can't license your own music if someone contacts you directly

**Option B — Non-Exclusive on Songtradr (35% commission):**

✅ **What you get:**
- You keep your music available everywhere simultaneously
- If someone contacts you directly, you keep 100%
- Freedom to add/remove songs anytime
- No lock-in period

❌ **What you give up:**
- No active pitching — you're waiting to be discovered
- Lower placement rates (Songtradr is marketplace pricing, not boutique)
- No personal relationship with supervisors on your behalf

**The math over 3 years (hypothetical):**

| Scenario | Placements | Avg Fee | Gross | Your Take |
|----------|-----------|---------|-------|----------|
| Exclusive agency (active pitching) | 8 placements | $5,000 | $40,000 | $20,000 (50%) |
| Songtradr (passive) | 3 placements | $2,000 | $6,000 | $3,900 (65%) |
| Hybrid (non-exclusive agency at 35%) | 5 placements | $3,500 | $17,500 | $11,375 (65%) |

**Advocate position:** The exclusive deal MIGHT generate more total revenue — but only if the agency is genuinely active. Ask them:
1. How many placements have they made in the last 12 months for similar catalogs?
2. Can they name specific supervisors they pitch to regularly?
3. Will they provide quarterly activity reports?
4. **CRITICAL: Negotiate the commission to 35-40%.** 50% is above market.
5. **Add a performance clause:** If they land fewer than 3 placements in the first year, you can terminate.
6. **Reduce term to 18 months** with option to renew.

Never sign an exclusive deal without a performance clause. An exclusive with no activity = your catalog in a drawer.

I want to sample a song from an artist who passed away 10 years ago. Their music is on streaming but I can't find who to contact for clearance. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Jordan","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":70000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"licensing"}]

Jordan, licensing from a deceased artist's catalog requires identifying the current rights holders — which can be complex. Here's the systematic approach.

Step 1 — Identify the Current Rights Holders:
When an artist passes away, their copyrights transfer to their estate. However, the practical control often sits with:

1. Publishing: Search PRO databases (ASCAP ACE, BMI Repertoire) for the song. The publisher listed there is your first point of contact for composition clearance. The publisher may have changed since the artist's passing (catalogs are frequently bought and sold).

2. Master Recording: Check who distributes the music on streaming platforms. The distributor or label that currently controls the masters is your contact for master clearance. If the music is on a major label, contact the label's A&R or licensing department.

3. Estate: If neither PRO nor label databases yield results, you may need to contact the estate directly. Estate contact info can sometimes be found through: the artist's official website, social media accounts managed by the estate, entertainment attorneys who represented the artist, published obituaries or tributes that mention estate executors.

Let me run check_availability on the specific song to identify the current publisher and label. I can also use browser_tool to research the estate's management.

Step 2 — The Clearance Process:
Once identified, the process is the same as clearing any sample:
- Contact the publisher for composition clearance
- Contact the label/estate for master clearance
- Negotiate fees and terms
- Get written agreements before release

Potential Complications:
- Disputed estates: If multiple heirs are fighting over the estate, clearance may be impossible until legal resolution
- Orphan works: If the rights holders truly cannot be located, the work may be an 'orphan work' — this is a legal gray area. Do NOT use without clearance, even for orphan works.
- Re-issued catalogs: The original label may no longer control the masters. Check current distribution.

What's the song and artist name? I'll start the rights holder search.

How do I actually get my music in TV shows and movies? I hear about sync licensing but I have no idea how to break in. [Context: {"currentModule":"licensing","hasProject":true}]

Sync licensing is when your music is synchronized to visual media — TV, film, ads, video games, trailers. It's one of the highest-paying revenue streams for indie artists. Here's how to break in:

Understanding the deal: Every sync requires TWO licenses — Master license (for the recording, controlled by whoever owns the master — usually you if you're indie) and Sync license (for the composition, controlled by the songwriter/publisher). As a fully independent artist who writes and records your own music, you control BOTH — this is a massive advantage because music supervisors can clear your music with one email.

How music gets placed: (1) Music supervisors browse sync libraries and receive pitches from publishers/agents. (2) Supervisors search by mood, genre, tempo, lyrical theme — NOT by artist name or fame. (3) They need music that is 100% clearable with no unresolved splits or samples.

How to get started: (1) Register with sync libraries: Musicbed (selective, higher-end), Songtradr (open, non-exclusive), Artlist (subscription model), Music Gateway, Taxi (paid membership). (2) Create instrumental versions of every track — supervisors often need vocals-free versions. (3) Tag your music meticulously: mood, genre, tempo, instrumentation, lyrical themes. (4) Ensure your splits are documented and all contributors have signed off.

What pays: TV background placement (10-60 seconds): $500-$5,000. Featured TV placement (with vocals, emotional scene): $5,000-$25,000. Major ad campaign: $10,000-$100,000+. Indie film: $500-$3,000. Video game: $1,000-$25,000.

Pro tip: Join the Guild of Music Supervisors mailing list. Attend sync conferences (SXSW, A2IM Indie Week, Sync Summit). Build relationships — sync is a relationship business.
